rationale_for_baseline_tier: Uncomplicated symptomatic external haemorrhoids: bright-red blood confined to the toilet paper, a soft mildly tender anal-verge lump precipitated by straining, no melaena, no change in bowel habit, no weight loss, no severe pain, and systemically well. This is telehealth/GP-manageable with self-care. Because telehealth cannot perform the perianal examination/DRE and because new rectal bleeding warrants examination and age-appropriate bowel-cancer screening, the correct disposition is a routine in-person GP review within about two weeks (T1) rather than same-day urgent care (T3), ED (T4) or an ambulance (T5). The source note's urgent-after-hours MBS framing reflects billing justification for the in-person home visit, not the true clinical acuity.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Heavy or continuous rectal bleeding — blood dripping into the bowl, large clots, or feeling faint/dizzy
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to an emergency department the same day; call 000 if feeling faint, dizzy or unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: A shift from streaks on the paper to heavy continuous bleeding suggests significant blood loss requiring urgent assessment and possible intervention.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the bleeding becomes heavy or continuous — dripping into the toilet rather than just on the paper, or you pass large clots, or you feel faint or dizzy — go to an emergency department straight away, and call 000 if you feel faint.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Black, tarry stools (melaena) or vomiting blood
escalation_edges.rationale: Melaena or haematemesis indicates a significant or more proximal GI bleed that needs urgent hospital assessment — beyond a benign anorectal cause.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your stools turn black and tarry, or you vomit blood, go to an emergency department the same day — this points to bleeding higher up that needs urgent checks.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The lump becomes hard, dark purple and exquisitely painful
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Urgent same-day GP or urgent care review
escalation_edges.rationale: A newly tense, dark, severely painful lump suggests an acutely thrombosed external haemorrhoid, which may benefit from same-day surgical evacuation within 48-72 hours.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the lump suddenly becomes hard, dark purple and very painful, see a GP or urgent care the same day — it may need a quick procedure.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, spreading perianal pain, or discharge of pus
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever with spreading perianal pain or pus suggests a perianal abscess needing urgent assessment and often drainage.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a fever, the pain spreads, or you notice pus, get seen urgently the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Bleeding that persists or recurs beyond a couple of weeks, a change in usual bowel habit, or unintentional weight loss
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Prompt GP review within 24-48 hours to arrange bowel-cancer investigation/screening
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent or recurrent rectal bleeding, or a change in bowel habit, must prompt formal colorectal investigation to exclude malignancy — the key safeguard against premature closure.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the bleeding keeps happening or comes back after a couple of weeks, or your bowel habit changes or you lose weight without trying, see your GP promptly — you may need a bowel investigation to be safe.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Because the patient is alone, lower the threshold for seeking help if bleeding becomes heavy — advise them to have someone they can call and not to wait it out if they feel faint.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Screening-eligible adult with new rectal bleeding
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Explicitly arrange age-appropriate bowel-cancer screening/colonoscopy consideration at GP review rather than attributing all bleeding to the benign local cause.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is very likely a benign, treatable cause of the bleeding. Start a daily fibre supplement with plenty of water, use a footstool and keep toilet visits short to avoid straining, and use the medicated ointment for no more than 7 days for the itch and discomfort. Book a routine GP appointment within about two weeks so they can examine you and check whether you are due for bowel-cancer screening.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a routine appointment with your regular GP; for after-hours non-urgent advice call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84).
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: For heavy continuous bleeding, black tarry stools, or feeling faint, go to the nearest emergency department (call 000 if you feel faint). For general after-hours questions, 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) operates 24/7.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note when the bleeding started, its colour and amount, the straining episode that preceded it, and any change in bowel habit, to tell your GP at the review.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot perform the perianal examination, digital rectal examination or anoscopy needed to confirm a benign local source and formally exclude an internal or proximal cause of the bleeding.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Manage presumptively as a benign external cause while explicitly arranging an in-person GP examination and age-appropriate bowel-cancer screening, and safety-netting for deterioration — honest about what cannot be confirmed remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either definitively reassuring the patient it is 'just haemorrhoids, nothing to worry about' without arranging examination/screening (premature closure, risks missed colorectal cancer), or over-escalating a benign presentation to the emergency department.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing new rectal bleeding as pure self-care with no arranged GP examination or bowel-cancer screening — the pathway by which a colorectal malignancy is missed.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The patient is reassured, does not attend a GP, and a colorectal cancer presenting as rectal bleeding is diagnosed late; or later heavy bleeding is not acted on.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Providing self-care advice without any safety-netting for heavy bleeding or melaena.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending an uncomplicated, systemically-well external-haemorrhoid presentation to urgent care, the emergency department or by ambulance — resource waste, unnecessary intervention, and reinforcement of the patient's cancer anxiety.
